content.text: A robot that can navigate around furniture is not yet ready for public spaces. Pedestrians are not static obstacles — they move, change direction, form groups, cross streets, and follow complex social norms that vary by culture and context. A robot that treats people as furniture-shaped obstacles will cut through groups, pass too close, behave erratically, and inspire distrust. Pedestrian interaction is the discipline of making robots aware of human motion patterns, intentions, and social expectations so they can move in a way that feels natural, predictable, and safe to everyone sharing the space.
What is it?

Pedestrian interaction covers the perception, prediction, and planning capabilities a robot needs to navigate safely and socially acceptably among people. It includes: (1) detecting and tracking individual pedestrians; (2) predicting where each person will be in the next 1–5 seconds; (3) recognising their intent (crossing the street, stopping, walking toward the robot); (4) making yield/proceed decisions in interaction scenarios; and (5) executing those decisions in a way that is legible — that is, comprehensible to the human so they know what the robot is doing and can adjust their own behaviour accordingly.
The field draws heavily on two foundational human behaviour sciences:
proxemics
(the study of the culturally-determined interpersonal distances humans maintain) and
social norms for navigation
(right-hand rule in corridors, yielding to groups, slowing when approaching a person who has not seen you). Robots that violate these norms cause discomfort even without physical contact.
Why it matters

Autonomous vehicles must yield to pedestrians at crossings — this is not optional, it is a legal requirement in every jurisdiction. A robot that miscalculates TTC (time-to-collision) or fails to recognise a pedestrian's intention to cross can cause a fatality. Indoor service robots (hospital delivery, hotel logistics, warehouse AMRs) share corridors with people and must not block or intimidate them. Social robot navigation is therefore both a safety concern and an adoption concern — robots that make people feel unsafe will be removed from deployment regardless of their technical capability.

The technical challenge is that pedestrians are non-cooperative agents: unlike vehicles that follow lane markings, pedestrians may suddenly reverse direction, stop to look at their phone, or jaywalk. Prediction must be probabilistic and multi-modal — the robot must consider multiple plausible futures simultaneously rather than committing to a single trajectory forecast.
Benefits
Predictive models allow the robot to start decelerating before a collision risk becomes critical, giving both the robot and the pedestrian time to react.
Proxemics-aware planning produces paths that maintain socially comfortable distances without being unnecessarily wide — enabling navigation in dense spaces.
Legible robot motion — signalling intent via trajectory shape and speed — reduces the uncertainty that causes pedestrians to freeze or make sudden evasive moves.
IRL-learned social norms generalise across environments better than hand-coded rule sets — the robot learns from observed human navigation data.
Multi-modal prediction (Trajectron++) surfaces scenarios where a pedestrian might turn left or right with different probabilities, letting the planner hedge with a path that is safe under both modes.
Trade-offs & limitations
High-quality prediction models (Trajectron++, Wayformer) require GPU inference and have latency of 20–100 ms per batch, which may not fit in tight control loops.
Social Force Model is fast and interpretable but assumes a fixed behavioural model; it fails in dense crowds, narrow passages, and group dynamics it was not calibrated for.
Intent recognition is inherently uncertain — a pedestrian stepping off the kerb may or may not intend to cross; false yields waste time and cause confusion.
Proxemics norms vary by culture — personal space expectations in Tokyo differ from those in New York, and a single calibration may not transfer.
Overly cautious robots get "frozen" in crowds — the Freezing Robot Problem occurs when the planner can find no safe path and stops indefinitely.
TTC is a scalar metric — it cannot capture situations where two pedestrians' paths are not converging but still create a narrow corridor the robot must thread.
How it works
The
Social Force Model (SFM)

, introduced by Helbing and Molnar (1995), describes pedestrian motion as the sum of attractive and repulsive Newtonian forces. The desired velocity vector pulls each pedestrian toward their goal; repulsive forces push them away from walls and other pedestrians. The resulting ordinary differential equation is numerically integrated forward in time to produce predicted trajectories. SFM is cheap (O(n^2) per step for n pedestrians) and easy to implement, but cannot model goal-directed behaviour changes like stopping to talk.
Deep learning models treat prediction as a sequence-to-sequence problem.
Social LSTM

(Alahi et al. 2016) processes each pedestrian's past trajectory with an LSTM, then shares hidden states between nearby pedestrians via a social pooling layer. The model predicts a Gaussian distribution over future positions.
Trajectron++

(Salzmann et al. 2021) models the scene as a dynamic graph where agents are nodes and their interactions are edges. A Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) generates multi-modal trajectory distributions — i.e., the model can produce a set of K plausible future trajectories with associated probabilities, capturing the inherent multi-modality of human motion.
TTC (time-to-collision) is the simplest risk metric for the yield/proceed decision. For a pedestrian at distance d moving toward the robot at relative speed v_rel:

If TTC < threshold (typically 2–4 s for urban driving), the robot yields. More sophisticated decisions use a risk score that integrates over predicted trajectory distributions: risk is proportional to the probability mass of future positions that intersect the robot's planned path.

Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) learns a reward function from observed human navigation demonstrations. Given a dataset of human trajectories (e.g., ETH/UCY pedestrian datasets), IRL finds the reward weights that make the observed trajectories optimal under the learned policy. This reward encodes social norms without the engineer having to specify them explicitly. The robot then plans using the inferred reward, naturally producing socially normative behaviour.
Architecture overview

The full pedestrian interaction pipeline is shown below: from sensor input through detection/tracking, trajectory prediction, intent recognition, and yield/proceed decisions to an interaction-aware planner that produces robot velocity commands.

attrs.alt: Pedestrian Interaction Pipeline: sensor input through detection/tracking, trajectory prediction (SFM/LSTM/Trajectron++), intent recognition, yield/proceed decision to interaction-aware planner

content.text: When to use it
Use SFM-based interaction for real-time indoor service robots with ≤10 pedestrians in view — it is fast, tunable, and does not require training data.
Use Social LSTM or Trajectron++ when you have pedestrian trajectory data from your deployment environment and need accurate multi-step prediction for an autonomous vehicle or drone.
Use TTC-based yield logic for crosswalk or corridor yield decisions where a simple risk scalar is sufficient and must run at 20+ Hz.
Use IRL or GAIL when you need the robot to learn social norms from demonstration data rather than engineering them by hand — especially for novel social contexts.
Use ORCA (Optimal Reciprocal Collision Avoidance) for multi-agent collision avoidance in simulation environments (Gazebo, CARLA, Isaac Sim) where you can run many agents simultaneously.
How to implement

A practical implementation starts with the Social Force Model for pedestrian simulation — it requires only positions and velocities as state, has no training requirements, and runs in real time. The key parameters are the desired speed
v0
, relaxation time
tau
, and the repulsive force parameters
A
(strength) and
B
(range). Typical values are A = 2000 N, B = 0.08 m for pedestrian–pedestrian, calibrated from the original Helbing paper.
Examples
Example 1: Social Force Model pedestrian simulation in Python

import numpy as np
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

# ---- Social Force Model (Helbing & Molnar 1995) ----
# Units: SI (metres, seconds, Newtons)

A_REPULSE = 2000.0   # N: repulsive force strength
B_REPULSE = 0.08     # m: repulsive force range
A_WALL    = 2000.0   # N: wall repulsion strength
B_WALL    = 0.08     # m: wall repulsion range

@dataclass
class Pedestrian:
    pos:        np.ndarray   # (2,) position in metres
    vel:        np.ndarray   # (2,) current velocity m/s
    goal:       np.ndarray   # (2,) desired destination
    v0:         float = 1.3  # desired speed m/s
    tau:        float = 0.5  # relaxation time s
    mass:       float = 70.0 # kg

def acceleration(ped: Pedestrian,
                 others: list['Pedestrian'],
                 walls: list[tuple[np.ndarray, np.ndarray]]) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Compute the social force acceleration for one pedestrian.
    walls: list of (point_on_wall, normal_pointing_away) pairs.
    """
    # 1. Self-driven force toward goal
    to_goal = ped.goal - ped.pos
    dist_to_goal = np.linalg.norm(to_goal)
    if dist_to_goal > 0.1:
        e_desired = to_goal / dist_to_goal  # unit vector toward goal
    else:
        e_desired = np.zeros(2)
    f_drive = ped.mass * (ped.v0 * e_desired - ped.vel) / ped.tau

    # 2. Repulsive forces from other pedestrians
    f_social = np.zeros(2)
    for other in others:
        r_ij = ped.pos - other.pos
        d_ij = np.linalg.norm(r_ij)
        if d_ij < 1e-6:
            continue
        e_ij = r_ij / d_ij  # unit vector from other to self
        # Exponential repulsion
        f_social += A_REPULSE * np.exp(-d_ij / B_REPULSE) * e_ij

    # 3. Repulsive forces from walls
    f_wall = np.zeros(2)
    for w_point, w_normal in walls:
        w_normal = w_normal / np.linalg.norm(w_normal)
        d_wall = np.dot(ped.pos - w_point, w_normal)
        if d_wall > 0:
            f_wall += A_WALL * np.exp(-d_wall / B_WALL) * w_normal

    return (f_drive + f_social + f_wall) / ped.mass

def step(pedestrians: list[Pedestrian],
         walls: list, dt: float = 0.05) -> None:
    """Euler integration step for all pedestrians."""
    accs = [
        acceleration(p, [q for q in pedestrians if q is not p], walls)
        for p in pedestrians
    ]
    for p, a in zip(pedestrians, accs):
        p.vel = p.vel + a * dt
        # Clip to maximum speed 2x desired
        spd = np.linalg.norm(p.vel)
        if spd > 2 * p.v0:
            p.vel = p.vel * (2 * p.v0 / spd)
        p.pos = p.pos + p.vel * dt

# ---- Simulate 2 pedestrians crossing paths ----
p1 = Pedestrian(pos=np.array([0.0, 0.0]),
                vel=np.array([0.0, 0.0]),
                goal=np.array([5.0, 0.0]))
p2 = Pedestrian(pos=np.array([5.0, 0.5]),
                vel=np.array([0.0, 0.0]),
                goal=np.array([0.0, 0.5]))

T, dt = 10.0, 0.05
for _ in range(int(T / dt)):
    step([p1, p2], walls=[])

print(f"p1 final: {p1.pos}")
print(f"p2 final: {p2.pos}")
Example 2: TTC-based pedestrian yield decision

import numpy as np
from typing import NamedTuple

class PedestrianState(NamedTuple):
    pos: np.ndarray    # (2,) metres, world frame
    vel: np.ndarray    # (2,) m/s

class RobotState(NamedTuple):
    pos: np.ndarray    # (2,) metres
    vel: np.ndarray    # (2,) m/s
    radius: float = 0.3  # metres

YIELD_TTC_THRESHOLD = 3.0     # seconds: yield if TTC below this
MIN_SAFE_DISTANCE   = 0.8     # metres: hard stop if below this
PED_RADIUS          = 0.3     # metres

def compute_ttc(robot: RobotState,
                ped: PedestrianState) -> float:
    """
    Compute time-to-collision assuming straight-line motion.
    Returns np.inf if paths don't converge.
    """
    rel_pos = ped.pos - robot.pos
    rel_vel = ped.vel - robot.vel  # relative velocity of ped w.r.t. robot

    # Combined radius for collision
    combined_r = robot.radius + PED_RADIUS

    # Solve quadratic |rel_pos + t*rel_vel|^2 = combined_r^2
    a = np.dot(rel_vel, rel_vel)
    if a < 1e-8:
        return np.inf  # no relative motion
    b = 2 * np.dot(rel_pos, rel_vel)
    c = np.dot(rel_pos, rel_pos) - combined_r ** 2

    discriminant = b ** 2 - 4 * a * c
    if discriminant < 0:
        return np.inf  # paths don't collide

    t1 = (-b - np.sqrt(discriminant)) / (2 * a)
    t2 = (-b + np.sqrt(discriminant)) / (2 * a)

    if t2 < 0:
        return np.inf  # collision already passed
    return max(0.0, t1) if t1 >= 0 else t2

def yield_decision(robot: RobotState,
                   pedestrians: list[PedestrianState],
                   current_speed: float) -> dict:
    """
    Returns recommended robot speed and yield flag.
    """
    min_ttc = np.inf
    min_dist = np.inf

    for ped in pedestrians:
        ttc  = compute_ttc(robot, ped)
        dist = np.linalg.norm(ped.pos - robot.pos)
        min_ttc  = min(min_ttc, ttc)
        min_dist = min(min_dist, dist)

    if min_dist < MIN_SAFE_DISTANCE:
        return {"action": "STOP",     "target_speed": 0.0, "ttc": min_ttc}
    elif min_ttc < YIELD_TTC_THRESHOLD:
        # Decelerate proportionally: slowest at TTC=0, fastest at TTC=threshold
        factor = min_ttc / YIELD_TTC_THRESHOLD
        return {"action": "YIELD",    "target_speed": current_speed * factor, "ttc": min_ttc}
    else:
        return {"action": "PROCEED",  "target_speed": current_speed, "ttc": min_ttc}

# ---- Demo ----
robot = RobotState(pos=np.array([0.0, 0.0]),
                   vel=np.array([0.5, 0.0]))
peds = [
    PedestrianState(pos=np.array([2.0, 0.5]),
                    vel=np.array([-0.3, -0.4])),  # walking toward robot
    PedestrianState(pos=np.array([5.0, 3.0]),
                    vel=np.array([0.0, -0.2])),    # far away
]
decision = yield_decision(robot, peds, current_speed=0.5)
print(f"Decision: {decision['action']}  target_speed={decision['target_speed']:.2f}  TTC={decision['ttc']:.2f}s")
Example 3: Simple trajectory prediction using constant velocity model and uncertainty propagation

import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import multivariate_normal

def predict_trajectories(
    pos: np.ndarray,      # (2,) current position
    vel: np.ndarray,      # (2,) current velocity
    sigma_vel: float,     # velocity noise standard deviation m/s
    horizon: float = 3.0, # seconds
    dt: float = 0.5,      # prediction time step
    n_samples: int = 100, # Monte Carlo samples for distribution
) -> list[np.ndarray]:    # list of (T, 2) trajectory arrays
    """
    Constant-velocity prediction with Gaussian velocity noise.
    Returns n_samples predicted trajectories over [dt, horizon].
    This is a simplified stand-in for a full deep learning model.
    """
    T = int(horizon / dt)
    rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

    # Sample velocity perturbations
    vel_noise = rng.normal(0, sigma_vel, size=(n_samples, 2))
    perturbed_vels = vel[None, :] + vel_noise  # (n_samples, 2)

    trajectories = []
    for pv in perturbed_vels:
        traj = np.zeros((T, 2))
        p = pos.copy()
        v = pv.copy()
        for t in range(T):
            p = p + v * dt
            # Add growing positional uncertainty
            p += rng.normal(0, 0.05 * (t + 1), size=2)
            traj[t] = p
        trajectories.append(traj)
    return trajectories

def collision_probability(
    robot_path: np.ndarray,  # (T, 2)
    trajectories: list[np.ndarray],
    combined_radius: float = 0.6,
) -> np.ndarray:  # (T,) probability of collision at each step
    """
    Estimates collision probability at each time step by counting
    sample trajectories that intersect the robot path circle.
    """
    T = robot_path.shape[0]
    n = len(trajectories)
    probs = np.zeros(T)
    for t in range(T):
        robot_pos = robot_path[t]
        in_collision = sum(
            np.linalg.norm(traj[t] - robot_pos) < combined_radius
            for traj in trajectories
        )
        probs[t] = in_collision / n
    return probs

# ---- Demo ----
ped_pos = np.array([3.0, 0.4])
ped_vel = np.array([-0.5, 0.0])  # walking toward robot
trajs = predict_trajectories(ped_pos, ped_vel, sigma_vel=0.2)

# Robot straight path at 0.5 m/s
T = 6
robot_path = np.array([[0.5 * t * 0.5, 0.0] for t in range(T)])
probs = collision_probability(robot_path, trajs)
for t, p in enumerate(probs):
    print(f"t={t * 0.5:.1f}s: collision probability = {p:.2%}")
Real-world example

Waymo's autonomous vehicles use a combination of 3-D pedestrian detection (Waymo Open Dataset contains 22 million labelled pedestrian bounding boxes), tracking with Kalman filtering, and a learned motion prediction model that produces multi-modal trajectory distributions over a 8-second horizon. At crosswalks, the vehicle's behavior planner evaluates the probability that the pedestrian will complete crossing before the vehicle's planned path. If P(crossing) > threshold and TTC < margin, the vehicle decelerates to allow the crossing. This is called a
conditional yield
and is more conservative than a simple TTC threshold because it accounts for prediction uncertainty.

Hospital delivery robots (e.g., Aethon TUG, Savioke Relay) use Nav2 with a social costmap layer that inflates the cost around detected humans beyond the normal footprint inflation. This effectively adds a social buffer zone that the planner avoids, implementing proxemics without an explicit social force calculation.
Common mistakes
Treating pedestrians as static obstacles in the global planner — the global planner's costmap should not be updated with pedestrian positions; that is the local planner's job.
Using only Euclidean distance to trigger yields — TTC accounts for velocity direction; a fast pedestrian moving away is safe while a slow one moving toward is not.
Running SFM with fixed parameters regardless of crowd density — SFM parameters calibrated for open corridors break down in dense crowds and can cause agents to overlap.
Ignoring tracking ID consistency — if tracking IDs are swapped between frames, the trajectory history used by prediction models becomes corrupted and produces nonsensical forecasts.
Over-predicting danger — too-conservative yields cause the robot to stop unnecessarily, irritating users and reducing throughput.
Not testing the Freezing Robot Problem — in simulation with dense virtual crowds, verify the robot can make forward progress; if it cannot, the social force weight is too high.
Best practices
Implement a social costmap layer in Nav2 that adds a decaying cost function around detected people — this integrates with the existing planner without requiring a custom social force implementation.
Use constant-velocity or linear prediction as a fast fallback when GPU-based models are unavailable; upgrade to learned models for critical scenarios (crosswalks, narrow corridors).
Tune TTC thresholds for your robot's maximum deceleration — a robot that decelerates at 0.5 m/s² needs a larger TTC margin than one that stops instantly.
Evaluate prediction models on the ETH/UCY pedestrian datasets as a standard benchmark before deployment — they cover indoor corridors and outdoor plazas with varying crowd densities.
Make robot intent legible: use slight directional cues (e.g., steering slightly before turning, decelerating smoothly before stopping) so pedestrians can anticipate robot behaviour.
Integrate a pedestrian prediction horizon appropriate to your speed — at 0.5 m/s you need 2–3 s of prediction; at 10 m/s (AV speed) you need 5–8 s.
Performance & scalability

SFM runs at O(n^2) per integration step; for n = 50 pedestrians at dt = 0.05 s this requires ~50k force evaluations per second — well within CPU capability. Social LSTM inference on a single Tesla V100 takes ~5 ms per batch of 20 pedestrians for a 2-second horizon. Trajectron++ takes ~20 ms per scene on the same GPU. Both are compatible with a 10 Hz prediction loop when run asynchronously.

Pedestrian detection (YOLOv8 + DeepSORT) adds ~15 ms at 640×480 on a Jetson Orin GPU. The complete pedestrian interaction pipeline — detect, track, predict (SFM), decide — runs at 20 Hz on an embedded GPU, which is sufficient for robot speeds below 2 m/s. For faster robots or AV applications, a dedicated perception computer running at 50–100 Hz is standard.

Security & reliability

Pedestrian prediction models can be fooled by adversarial trajectories. An attacker who knows a deployed Social LSTM's parameters could walk in a pattern that maximises the predicted probability of a safe path, causing the robot to proceed when it should yield. More practically, prediction models trained on ETH/UCY fail silently on crowd densities or pedestrian behaviours not in their training distribution. Reliability requires monitoring prediction confidence and falling back to a conservative hard-TTC threshold when confidence is low.

Ethical considerations are also relevant. A robot that routinely yields more readily to adults than to children (due to detection model bias trained on adult-skewed data) creates unequal safety guarantees. Pedestrian interaction systems should be audited for demographic bias in detection accuracy, and yield thresholds should be set conservatively enough to be safe even for pedestrians the detector misclassifies.
Key takeaways
Pedestrian interaction requires prediction, intent recognition, and social norm compliance — treating people as static obstacles is insufficient and unsafe.
SFM is the classical fast baseline: interpretable, real-time, no training needed, but limited to simplified crowd dynamics.
Deep models (Social LSTM, Trajectron++) predict multi-modal future distributions — essential for yielding decisions where pedestrian intent is ambiguous.
TTC is the most practical yield decision metric: computationally cheap, intuitively tunable, and accounts for both distance and approach velocity.
Proxemics zones define the socially acceptable minimum approach distance; robots that enter personal space cause discomfort regardless of collision risk.
IRL learns social norms from demonstration data — preferred when explicit rule encoding would miss implicit cultural norms.
The Freezing Robot Problem is a critical failure mode: validate that the robot can make forward progress in dense simulated crowds before deployment.
Audit prediction models for demographic bias and set conservative yield thresholds to account for misdetection of children, wheelchair users, and other groups.
